questions.prompt: A field accepts ages 18–60. Which is the correct 2-value BVA set?
questions.explanation: Two values per boundary: the boundary itself and its nearest invalid neighbour — 17 & 18 at the low edge, 60 & 61 at the high edge.

questions.prompt: Why do bugs cluster at boundaries?
options: Inputs near the edge are larger and slower to process
Developers make off-by-one mistakes — using > instead of >=, or < instead of <=
Boundary values are harder for users to type
Compilers round numbers near the edges
questions.explanation: Off-by-one operator mistakes are invisible everywhere except right at the boundary.

questions.prompt: What is the 3-value BVA set for a field accepting 18–60?
questions.explanation: 3-value BVA adds the nearest valid neighbour inside the range: 17,18,19 at the low edge and 59,60,61 at the high edge.

questions.prompt: A tester checks 25, 40 and 55 against the 18–60 field. What is missing, and what is the risk?
options: Nothing is missing; three values is enough coverage
Boundary Value Analysis is missing — off-by-one bugs at 17/18 and 60/61 would go undetected
Equivalence Partitioning is missing — the valid range is untested
Load testing is missing — the field may be slow
questions.explanation: All three values sit in the middle of the valid partition. Without testing the edges, off-by-one defects at the boundaries escape.

questions.prompt: True or false: Boundary Value Analysis applies only to numeric fields.
questions.explanation: False — anything with an ordered range has boundaries: string lengths, dates, file sizes, list counts, timeouts.

questions.prompt: Signup allows ages 18–100. QA tested only 18 and 100. Production then accepted 101. Which BVA rule was skipped?
options: Testing the middle of the valid range
Testing the invalid neighbours just outside the range (17 and 101)
questions.explanation: They tested the boundaries themselves but not the invalid neighbours just outside — 17 and 101 — so the upper off-by-one slipped through.
